coredata.dc:title: N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots-graphene-TiO2 nanotubes composite with enhanced photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Hongwei
Shen, Kai
Hu, Xiaoying
Qiao, Liang
Zheng, Weitao
coredata.dc:description: N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) -reduced graphene oxide- (rGO) -TiO2 nanotubes (TiO2NT) composites were prepared by a facile alkaline hydrothermal reaction and physical stirring process. The apparent rate constant of the N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT composite, exhibiting the highest photodegradation efficiency, is 1.8 times and 16.3 times higher than those of 10%rGO + TiO2NT and pure TiO2NT, respectively, for the degradation of methyl orange (MO) under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm). Our work could provide new insights into the fabrication of GQDs-semiconductor composites as high performance photocatalysts and broaden the range of doped GQDs widely used for environmental protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots
Graphene
TiO2 nanotubes
Photocatalytic activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N, S-GQDs + rGO + TiO2NT

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Finance of Jilin Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from the NSF (Nos. 51472106 , 51202017 and 51372095 ) of China and the NSF (Nos. 20140101107JC and 20140101092JC ) of Jilin Province are highly appreciated. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (A): (a) TiO2NT (b) 5%rGO + TiO2NT (c) 10%rGO + TiO2NT. (d) 15%rGO + TiO2NT and partial magnified detail of panel A about the 23–28° region. XRD pattern of (B): (e) N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT.
Raman spectrum of Characteristic D and G bands of (b) 5%rGO + TiO2NT (c) 10%rGO + TiO2NT (d) 15%rGO + TiO2NT (e) N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT composite and (f) GO.
SEM images of (A) GO and (B) N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT composite.
(A) TEM image of N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT (B) inner and outside diameter of TiO2NT (C) HRTEM image of TiO2 and N, S-GQDs.
High-resolution XPS of (A) C 1s of GO and (B) C 1s of 10%rGO + TiO2NT, XPS full survey of (C) N, S-GQDs and (D) N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT.
(A) UV–vis absorption of N, S-GQDs (B) UV–vis absorption of (a) TiO2NT (b) 5%rGO + TiO2NT (c) 10%rGO + TiO2NT (d) 15%rGO + TiO2NT and (e) N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT.

(A) The photocatalytic activities and (B) the linear fittings of the pseudo-first-order kinetics model in degrading methyl orange under visible light (λ > 400 nm) irradiation for different catalysts: (a) TiO2NT (b) 5%rGO + TiO2NT (c) 15%rGO + TiO2NT (d) 10%rGO + TiO2NT and (e) N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of N, S-GQDs + rGO + TiO2NT ternary composite.
The possible photocatalytic mechanism for the N, S-GQDs + rGO + TiO2NT under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots-graphene-TiO2 nanotubes composite with enhanced photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Key Laboratory of Automobile Materials of MOE, Jilin University, Changchun, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Key Laboratory of Automobile Materials of MOE

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Changchun University, Changchun, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) -reduced graphene oxide- (rGO) -TiO2 nanotubes (TiO2NT) composites were prepared by a facile alkaline hydrothermal reaction and physical stirring process. The apparent rate constant of the N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT composite, exhibiting the highest photodegradation efficiency, is 1.8 times and 16.3 times higher than those of 10%rGO + TiO2NT and pure TiO2NT, respectively, for the degradation of methyl orange (MO) under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm). Our work could provide new insights into the fabrication of GQDs-semiconductor composites as high performance photocatalysts and broaden the range of doped GQDs widely used for environmental protection.

ce:keyword.ce:text: N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few decades, the photocatalytic degradation of organic dye has gained more and more attention. Due to its strong oxidation resistance, good stability, and low toxicity, titanium dioxide is the most widely used photocatalyst in the industry [1]. However, photogenerated electrons and holes recombine quickly due to the wide band gap of titanium dioxide. This material only functions under UV light irradiation with a wavelength shorter than 387 nm, which only covers 4% of the solar spectrum. The efficient use of sunlight becomes an appealing challenge for photocatalyst development [2]. Zhang and coworkers prepared a P25-graphene composite and reported the efficient use of sunlight [3]. Therefore, it can be assumed that graphene is an ideal catalyst carrier or ancillary [4].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), single or few layer graphene with a thickness of only a few nanometers, were reported as new type of quantum dots (QDs) with the unique properties inherent from both graphene and QDs [5]. GQDs combine characteristics arising from their graphitic structure, their carbogenic origin and their quantum nature [6]. Several reports showed improvements on photocatalytic activity of GQDs, including the catalysis of the GQDs [7], enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible-light irradiation [8], and higher photocatalytic rates of TiO2/GQDs complex systems compared to pure TiO2 under visible light for the degradation of methylene blue [9]. Li et al. showed catalysis of well-defined colloidal GQDs [10]. Ma et al. prepared GQDs/g-C3N4 composites and reported their enhanced photocatalytic activity than g-C3N4 [11]. Luo et al. showed that GQDs/Au exhibited higher electrocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution [12]. Furthermore, Sun and co-workers deciphered the catalytic mechanism of GQDs from the chemical structure [13]. As carbon catalytic materials, considerable effort has been made to develop diverse GQDs [14]. Most recently, doped GQDs were shown as an attractive approach to effectively tune intrinsic material properties including electronic characteristics, surface, and local chemical features. The N atom, having a comparable atomic size and five valence electrons to bond with carbon atoms, has been widely used for chemical doping of carbon nanomaterials [15,16]. Sim et al. prepared N-GQDs and reported their electrocatalytic activity [17]. Dai et al. showed that N-GQDs/TiO2 nanofibers exhibit high photocurrents and excellent stability [18]. Zou et al. designed a novel metal-free N-GQDs/g-C3N4 catalyst, which showed much better photocatalytic activity than pure g-C3N4 for H2 evolution from water splitting [19]. Teng and co-workers prepared N-GQDs/Pt as a co-catalyst and improved the catalytic activity [20]. Recently, some researchers successfully produced co-doped GQDs, such as N, S co-doped GQDs [21,22], and N, B co-doped GQDs [23], that showed excellent catalytic activity, and exhibited a smaller band gap than N doped GQDs resulting in absorption in the visible wavelength region [24]. However, N, S-GQDs have rarely been investigated on the degradation abilities of organic pollution. Compared with traditional metal semiconductor quantum dots used in photocatalysis, such as Cd2+ and W3+, N, S-GQDs will not cause secondary pollution and provides excellent properties of semiconductor quantum dots, which is the advantage of graphene.

Herein, we synthetized N, S-GQDs + rGO + TiO2NT ternary composites using a facile alkaline hydrothermal and physical stirring process and report the enhanced photocatalytic activity by degrading the MO. Considering the large surface area of TiO2NT, this composite provides effective area to receive sun light as well as vast active sites for electrons. The combination of large surface area from both TiO2NT and rGO may improve charge separation and stabilization. Due to the upconversion properties of GQDs, the N, S-GQDs composite can effectively suppress electron-hole recombination, extend the range of visible light, and improve the catalytic efficiency in the ternary composites.

Materials P25 (20% rutile and 80% anatase) was purchased from Degussa. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethanol (C2H6O), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) weight ratio 30% and dimethyl formamide (DMF) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). Citric acid and thiourea were supplied by Sinopharm chemical reagent (Shanghai, China). Graphite powder was purchased from Alfa Aesar China (Tianjin, China). All materials were used as received without further purification. Deionized (DI) water used in the preparation was from local sources.
Synthesis The preparation of N, S-GQDs + rGO + TiO2NT composite is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

0.3 g P25 powder in 30 ml NaOH (10 M) solution was stirred for 12 h without lighting. Then the solution was transferred into a 50 ml Teflon lined stainless autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 150 °C in an electric oven and kept for additional 24 h. A further DI water was added, and the resultant product centrifuged and washed with DI water repeatedly to adjust the pH to 7. And then 0.1 M HCl was added, and the resultant product centrifuged and washed with HCl repeatedly to adjust the pH to 1. Finally, DI water was added, and the resultant product centrifuged and washed with DI water repeatedly to adjust the pH to 7. The product was dried at 80 °C for 24 h.

GO was synthesized using a modified Hummer's method [25] (As shown in Supplementary materials, S1). The detail of the typical process can be referenced in the previous report [26]. The as-prepared GO and P25 powders by 5:95, 10:90 and 15:85 in different mass ratios were added to 10 M NaOH solution. The preparation method was followed by the synthesis of TiO2NT.

Citric acid (CA) (1 mmol) and thiourea (3 mmol) were dissolved into 8 ml DMF and stirred to form a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The solvothermal treatment was performed at 180 °C for 8 h. The products were cooled to room temperature, separated by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 15 min after adding ethanol into the solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S-GQDs + rGO + TiO2NT
ce:section.ce:para: The rGO + TiO2NT was dispersed into the N, S-GQD aqueous solution and kept stirring for 24 h. Then the N, S-GQDs + rGO + TiO2NT composites were collected by centrifugation at 8500 rpm for 30 min. The solid was dried at 70 °C for 24 h.

The as-prepared samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker, D8tools), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JOEL JSM-6700F), transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20), Raman spectroscopy (T64000 Horiba spectrometer), with a 633 nm Ar + laser excitation (a laser power of 7 mW), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer), with a hemisphere detector (an energy resolution of 0.1 eV supplied by an Al K α radiation source). UV–vis spectra was recorded using a Shimadzo UV-1601 PC spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) was collected by using a Biorad FTS-60A FTIR device equipped with a diffuse reflectance unit. The visible light was supplied by a 300 W Xe arc lamp (PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Perfect light Co., Ltd.)

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was evaluated through the degradation of MO in aqueous solution under visible light from a 300 W Xe arc lamp with a UV-CUT filter to cut off light of wavelength < 400 nm. A 20 mg of sample was added into 80 ml of MO solution (10 ppm). Prior to irradiation, photocatalysts were not subjected to any previous treatment and then were dispersed in MO solution under magnetic stirring in the dark for 30 min to ensure the establishment of adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Afterward the solution was exposed to the visible light irradiation. After a specific time interval (1 h), 4 ml solution was sampled and subjected to centrifugation to separate catalyst. The MO concentration was monitored by the absorbance value at the maximum peak using UV–vis scanning spectrophotometer.

Fig. 1 A shows the XRD pattern of the as-prepared rGO + TiO2NT with different weight ratios of rGO, including anatase and rutile phases. Furthermore, Fig. 1B displays the XRD pattern of N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT. The XRD pattern of GO can be found in the Supplementary materials, Fig. S2. The rGO + TiO2NT composites display a typical anatase phase with diffraction peaks corresponding to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220) and (215) crystal planes, respectively [26], while the rutile phase typically exhibits diffraction peaks corresponding to the (110) and (101) planes. The rGO + TiO2NT composites show similar XRD patterns as pure TiO2NT and no diffraction peaks of carbon species are observed in the composites, which is possibly due to the small amount of rGO. It is worth noting that the (101) peak for all samples gradually shifts to smaller angles from pure TiO2NT to the 15%rGO + TiO2NT composite with increasing rGO content, indicating that the lattice structure of TiO2 was distorted by the interaction with rGO. Fig. 1B show the XRD pattern of ternary composite. When the N, S-GQDs are added to the composite, still no clear diffraction patterns corresponding to carbon species can be observed (Fig. 1B). This observation may be attributed to the small amount and weak intensity of the GQDs, and furthermore, due to possible peak shielding of the main grapheme peak by anatase TiO2.

The Raman spectra depicting the characteristic D and G bands of carbon at 1353 cm−1 and 1591 cm−1 are shown in Fig. 2 , revealing differences between GO and the composite materials. The D band is a common feature for sp3 defects in carbon, and the G band provides information on in plane vibrations of sp2 bonded carbons. The intensity ratio of D to G usually reflects the order of defects in GO. The observed decreases in ID/IG ratios in the composites suggest that higher graphene contents don't reduce the average quantity of the sp2 domain [27]. By comparing the intensity ratios of D to G, we found that ID/IG of rGO + TiO2NT and the ternary composite are lower than that of GO, indicating a lower density of defects present in rGO. Moreover, the decrease in ID/IG ratios is a clear indication of the increase in the number of graphene layers [28]. Moreover, the blue shift of the G band can be attributed to the conversion of graphite to graphene sheets or the resonance of isolated double bonds at higher frequencies. Therefore, both the change in Raman band intensity and the blue shift of the G band provided clear evidence for the presence of graphene in the composites.

Fig. 3 A shows the SEM image of GO, in which GO appears as a piece of wave-like silk veils. Furthermore, as evident in Fig. 3B, the SEM image of the ternary composite shows that the TiO2NTs are anchored onto the rGO sheets after hydrothermal treatment. From Fig. 3B, we can see the morphology of the nanotubes intertwined with each other forming a porous sphere due to the formation of TiO2NTs clusters. The morphology of the nanotubes contributes to a larger surface area for light-harvesting than using nanoparticles. Due to the hydrothermal reaction, the oxygen functional groups of GO were disconnected and a chemical bond was formed between TiO2NTs and rGO [29]. The GO sheets were further cut hydrothermally under strong alkaline (pH > 12) conditions, resulting in the rGO surface being covered with an interconnected network of TiO2NTs. Therefore, no large slice structure of rGO was observed. A transparent rGO sheet featuring multiple wrinkles is inserted into the porous TiO2NT sphere allowing close contact between two components. The GQDs should be closely attached to TiO2NTs and rGO, however, due to the small particle size of GQDs a clear observation using SEM could not be achieved [5]. In addition, GQDs and rGO, having the same chemical structure, may not be easily identified when incorporated with each other. Therefore, more TEM data will be discussed in the following section.

The TEM images displayed in Fig. 4 reveal that the rGO sheets are covered with TiO2NTs and N, S-GQDs. It can be seen that TiO2NTs occupy most of the available surface area of rGO, providing a high loading of TiO2NTs in the composite. Furthermore, the N, S-GQDs are attached to the surface of rGO and TiO2NTs. As seen in Fig. 4B, TiO2NTs on the rGO surface show inner and outer diameters of 3.9 and 7.8 nm, respectively. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) imaging (Fig. 4C) reveals that the lattice space of 0.35 and 0.24 nm corresponds to TiO2 (101) and N, S-GQDs (1120), respectively, demonstrating the existence of TiO2NT and N, S-GQDs.

The FTIR spectra of GO and the composites (Supplementary materials, Fig. S3) show several strong characteristic absorption peaks during the oxidation process. The strong absorption peak at 3400 cm−1 is an extension of adsorbed water and the small peak at around 1730 cm−1 is assigned to the CO stretching of residual COOH groups. The absorption peak at 1650 cm−1 is the vibrational absorption peak of CC bonds. HO bonds appear near the 1370 cm−1 peak, whereas CO bonds become visible near the 1020 cm−1 peak. These absorption peaks confirm the existence of GO. Pure TiO2NT shows a low frequency band at around 400–1000 cm−1, which corresponds to the vibration of TiOTi bonds. There are two relatively strong absorption peaks near 3400 and 1600 cm−1. These absorption peaks confirm that water was absorbed on the surface of TiO2NTs. Water adsorption in the photocatalytic process further accelerates the photocatalytic reaction with strong oxidation ability of active oxygen radicals by TiO2 [30]. In addition, the FTIR spectrum of the ternary composite shows different characteristic peaks than TiO2NT and 10%rGO + TiO2NT. The asymmetric stretching vibrations of CS is found at 1182 cm−1 and the weak CS stretching is located at 786 cm−1. Furthermore, the stretching vibrations of CN are detected at 1101 cm−1. The bands at 1715 and 1650 cm−1 are attributed to the vibrational absorption band of CO in COOH and CONH. The stretching vibrations of NH shows broad absorption at 3000-3500 cm−1. This indicates the presence of amino groups on the surface of GQDs, which results in GQDs with good hydrophilic properties. The results above clearly indicate that the as-prepared GQDs are indeed N, S-GQDs. The FTIR spectrum of 10%rGO + TiO2NT and N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT after the photocatalytic reaction show the same characteristic absorption peaks as before the photocatalytic reaction as displayed in Fig. S5 (Supplementary materials).

Fig. 5 A shows the XPS spectrum of GO with the symmetric C 1s peak at 284.4 eV for CC bonds. Additionally, the oxygen functionalities attached to the carbons show peaks for CO (287.5 eV) and CO (289.1 eV) bonds at higher binding energies. The XPS data for the C 1s electrons of the 10%rGO + TiO2NT composite shows a dramatic decrease in the carbon−oxygen species in rGO (Fig. 5B). This suggests that the alkaline hydrothermal synthesis significantly reduces CO bonding, thereby converting GO to rGO. The full scan XPS spectrum of N, S-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 5C, presents five peaks at 163, 228, 285, 400, and 532 eV, which correspond to S 2p, S 2s, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. This indicates that the GQDs prepared from thiourea are doped with N and S atoms. Fig. 5D displays the full XPS survey spectrum of the N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT composite. The ternary composite contains Ti, O, C, N and S with the binding energies corresponding to Ti 2p, O 1s, C 1s, N 1s, S 1s and S 2p. The peaks at 458.6 and 464.5 eV are attributed to the binding energies of the Ti 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 electrons (Supplementary materials, Fig. S4A). The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Supplementary materials, Fig. S4B) shows that carbon atoms exist in three different chemical environments, corresponding to sp2C, sp3C and CO [31]. The sp2C peak found at 284.6 eV corresponds to graphene (CC). The sp3C peak located at 285.6 eV is attributed to CN, CS, and CO. The CO peak at 288.1 eV originates from carbonyls and carboxylates. The high-resolution N 1s and S 2p spectra of the ternary composite are shown in Fig. S4 (C, D) (Supplementary materials). These results imply that N, S-GQDs was incorporated into rGO + TiO2NT during the physical stirring process.

The UV–vis spectrum of N, S-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 6 A, displays absorption bands centered at 391, 464, 550 and 652 nm, respectively, which are similar to those of GQDs prepared by a hydrothermal graphene oxide reduction method [32]. We synthesized a series of N, S-GQDs by tuning the mole ratio of CA and thiourea. The elemental analysis is shown in Table 1. The UV–vis spectra of N, S-GQDs with different dopant amounts are shown in Fig. S6A (Supplementary materials). The origin of these peaks is related to the electron transition in heteroatom-containing GQDs. The absorption peak at 391 nm is due to the n→π* transition of the conjugated CO and CN bonds [33]. The absorption peak at 464 nm corresponds to the n→π* transition of the conjugated CN and CS bonds and the absorption peak at 550 nm is attributed to the n→π* transition of the conjugated CS bond. The absorption peak at 652 nm corresponds to the n→π* transition of the conjugated SO bond [34]. From Fig. 6A, we can conclude that the N, S-GQDs have a wide range of absorption capabilities for visible light in photodegradation processes.

As mentioned above, the light absorption range plays an important role in photocatalysis, especially for the visible light photodegradation of dyes [8]. As shown in Fig. 6B, with the increase of graphene oxide concentration, an obvious red shift of 40–50 nm in the absorption edge of rGO + TiO2NT powder can be observed. This indicates that the band gap of the composite is narrower [35]. This band gap narrowing should be attributed to the chemical bonding between TiO2NT and rGO, i.e. the formation of TiOC bonds, similar to carbon-doped TiO2 composites [36]. Therefore, a more efficient utilization of the solar spectrum can be achieved with the increase of the graphene oxide content. Due to the broad absorption band of N, S-GQDs, the ternary composite has a boarder absorption band than rGO + TiO2NT. However, the absorption bands of the ternary composites synthetized by different mole ratios of CA and thiourea show no obvious offset, in Fig. S6B (Supplementary materials). The visible light photoactivity of the N, S-GQDs + rGO + TiO2NT catalyst is expected to facilitate its application in practical environmental remediation.

The photocatalytic activities of TiO2NT, rGO + TiO2NT and N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT are measured by photodegradation of MO as model pollutant under visible light (λ > 400 nm) and the results are shown in Fig. 7 . The normalized temporal concentration changes (C/C0) of MO during the photodegradation are proportional to the normalized maximum absorbance (A/A0) and derive from the changes in the dye's absorption profile at a given time interval [37]. In the case of visible light photodegradation (Fig. 7A), TiO2NT shows rather poor photocatalytic activity due to its limited photoresponse range [38] and only 10% of the MO diminished after more than 2 h. It is clearly shown in Fig. 7A that the rGO + TiO2NT composites provide significant improvements for the photodegradation of MO compared to TiO2NT. In the composites with different contents of graphene, about 65%, 70% and 79% of MO was decomposed after 4 h. The kinetics of the photodegradation of TiO2NT, rGO + TiO2NT and N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT are fitted to a pseudo-first order reaction In(C0/C) = k × t, where k, C0 and C are the apparent rate constant, initial MO concentration and MO concentration after a certain time, respectively. The unit for the apparent rate constant is h−1. The designations ka, kb, kc, kd, ke correspond to the apparent rate constants of TiO2NT, 5%rGO + TiO2NT, 10%rGO + TiO2NT, 15%rGO + TiO2NT and N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT, respectively. The apparent rate constant of catalysts were shown in Fig. 7B. From this graph, it can be seen that the apparent rate constant of 10%rGO + TiO2NT is 0.3134 which is 8.9 times higher than pure TiO2NT. When the concentration of rGO reaches 10%, the binary catalyzed composite leads to the highest rate (0.3134) of photodegradation. It is noteworthy that the reason for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of 10%rGO + TiO2NT is the effective charge separation, stabilization and hindered recombination. Moreover, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the composite could be a result of the optimized amount of highly conductive rGO and strong coupling effect between TiO2NT and rGO layers [39]. Although higher concentrations of rGO can adsorb large quantities of pollutants, the light-harvesting surface of TiO2NT is reduced leading to lower photocatalytic performance. This effect is clearly observed in the 15%rGO + TiO2NT composite possessing excess rGO sheets, which partially block light irradiation. This strongly suggests that improved photocatalytic activity requires effective charge transfer between TiO2NT and rGO. Therefore, 10%rGO was chosen for the synthesis of the ternary composite.

Several reports proposed that GQDs/TiO2 composites provide excellent photocatalytic activity due to the interaction between GQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles [40]. We prepared GQDs and 10%rGO + TiO2NT composites by simply mixing them in a solution. The GQDs were absorbed on the surface of rGO + TiO2NT, which induced a color change from black to light brown for the ternary N, S-GQDs + rGO + TiO2NT composite. It is expected that combining GQDs with the binary catalyst in a ternary composite system can efficiently utilize the visible spectrum range of sunlight. Obviously, the ternary composite showed the highest photoactivity among all samples, whose photocatalytic degradation efficiency reached at 92% after 4 h under visible light irradiation. The apparent rate constant of the ternary composite is 0.5748 which is 1.8 and 16.3 times higher than 10%rGO + TiO2NT and TiO2NT, respectively. These results show that the photocatalytic activity of the composite strongly depends on the GQDs absorption capability of visible light. The excellent degradation ability of N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT is due to the broad absorption band from N, S-GQDs starting from 300 to 750 nm. This is also the reason why the N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT composite exhibits higher photocatalytic performance in comparison to TiO2NT and rGO + TiO2NT. We also measured the performance of ternary composites that were synthetized by different mole ratios of CA and thiourea. These three catalysts exhibit similar photodegradation efficiency as seen in Fig. S7 (Supplementary materials).

A possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism for ternary composite materials is illustrated in Scheme 2 . N, S-GQDs can absorb visible light efficiently. According to the proposed photocatalytic activity pathway of the N, S-GQDs composite, electrons in the valence band of GQDs can be excited to the conduction band and electron–hole pairs are generated [41]. Moreover, large aromatic domains of the GQDs create high affinity, good electrical conductivity and adsorptivity of MO. In addition, the large specific surface area of the GQDs also increases the light adsorption of the MO molecules. The TiO2 nanocrystal, as a n-type semiconductor, tends to accept the electrons and promotes the charge separation process [42]. The electrons are injected into the rGO + TiO2NT nanocrystals. This charge separation process promotes the formation of active oxygen (O2) and hydroxyl (OH) radicals. MO can be adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst and oxidized indirectly through hydroxyl radicals (OH) generated by the reaction of holes and the superoxide radical (O2) produced by the photogenerated electrons [43]. Therefore, during the photodegradation process of MO, the GQDs play an important role in enhancing the photocatalytic activity.

In summary, we prepared N, S-GQDs + rGO + TiO2NT composites by an alkaline hydrothermal and physical stirring process. This composite simultaneously exhibited an extended photoresponse range, enhanced charge separation and transportation properties. Based on these superior material properties, N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT demonstrated significant advancements over pure TiO2NT and rGO + TiO2NT for photodegradation of MO under visible light irradiation. The apparent rate constant of N, S-GQDs+10%rGO + TiO2NT is 1.8 and 16.3 times higher than those of 10%rGO + TiO2NT and pure TiO2NT, respectively. The N, S-GQDs + rGO + TiO2NT composite showed excellent photocatalytic performance indicating that GQDs can improve the utilization of solar light for energy conversion and environmental therapy. This work may offer insights for the preparation of high-performance multicomponent materials utilizing GQDs-based nanocomposites as photocatalyst for efficient solar energy utilization addressing various environmental issues.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the NSF (Nos. 51472106, 51202017 and 51372095) of China and the NSF (Nos. 20140101107JC and 20140101092JC) of Jilin Province are highly appreciated.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2016.08.261.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Titanium dioxide nanomaterials: synthesis, properties, modifications, and applications
P25-Graphene composite as a high performance photocatalyst
Waltzing with the versatile platform of graphene to synthesize composite photocatalysts
Graphene quantum dots: an emerging material for energy-related applications and beyond
Graphene quantum dots: in the crossroad of graphene, quantum dots and carbogenic nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Graphene quantum dots enhanced photocatalytic activity of zinc porphyrin toward the degradation of methylene blue under visible-light irradiation
Upconversion and downconversion fluorescent graphene quantum dots: ultrasonic preparation and photocatalysis
